design_philosophy.core_loop: Rapid button-mashing alternation to build speed/momentum, followed by well-timed action button presses for secondary mechanics (jumping angle, throw timing). Six distinct athletic events chain together with escalating difficulty.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Execution pressure: converting sustained manual input speed into competitive performance; qualifying time thresholds gate progression and demand increasing precision across multiple attempts per event.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Simulating Olympic athlete excellence through pure motor skill—prove mastery by beating qualifying standards across diverse track & field disciplines.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Converts athletic competition into pure reflexive execution challenge. No strategy, no resource management, no RNG—only hand speed, rhythm awareness, and timing discipline matter. Embraces arcade cabinet wear (trackball replacements indicate design expected extreme button wear). Multiplayer mode is *competitive but not comparative*: you don't race each other directly; you race qualifying times independently, emphasizing personal performance over head-to-head drama.

difficulty.lives_system: Limited lives pool; each failed qualification (one heat for running events, three attempts for other events) costs one life. Game ends when lives depleted.
difficulty.checkpointing: Six-event structure with qualifying thresholds; no mid-event checkpoints. Progression gated entirely by meeting minimum performance standards.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal. Qualifying times are fixed and escalate each loop cycle. Player can earn bonus lives via point accumulation (every 100,000 points), creating soft difficulty adjustment through extended play opportunity, but no difficulty selection menu documented.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Qualifying time thresholds unlock next event in sequence. Successfully completing all six events triggers higher-difficulty replay loop with elevated qualifying times.
replayability_hooks: Multi-loop difficulty escalation (world records increase each cycle)
Multiplayer competitive leaderboard potential via arcade cabinets
Six distinct event mechanics encourage skill specialization and mastery
Point accumulation toward bonus lives extends session length

world_building_techniques: 1984 Summer Olympics licensing (Japan Famicom version) provides thematic context
Visual scoreboard showing world records anchors Olympic competition fantasy
Packed audience backdrop establishes stadium atmosphere without explicit narrative

environmental_storytelling: Scoreboard displays world records, positioning player as competitor against historical Olympic standards rather than narrative antagonists
Stadium audience visual presence creates social/performative context for athletic achievement
Multi-player arcade cabinet setup (two control sets, four player slots) treats absent competitors as 'CPU' opponents—emphasizes personal qualification over social narrative

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Track & Field NES article)
